past_medical_history: Splenectomy following motor vehicle accident at age 32
Hypertension, diagnosed 8 years ago, well controlled on medication
Type 2 diabetes mellitus, diagnosed 5 years ago, managed with oral medications
Chronic sinusitis with two prior episodes requiring antibiotics in the past 3 years
Immunization status: received pneumococcal vaccine (Pneumovax 23) at time of splenectomy but has not received a booster or PCV20

medications.indication: Started 3 days ago by PCP for presumed sinusitis with headache and low-grade fever

family_history: Father with type 2 diabetes and coronary artery disease
Mother with hypertension, alive at age 82
No family history of neurological disease or immunodeficiency

social_history.smoking: Former smoker, quit 10 years ago, 15 pack-year history
social_history.living_situation: Lives with wife in a single-story home
social_history.exercise: Walks 2-3 miles three times per week

neurological_exam.mental_status: Alert but sluggish in responses. Oriented to person and place but states the date incorrectly by two days. Intermittently confused, asking the same question twice during the interview. Attention is impaired — unable to perform serial sevens (stops after two attempts) and spells WORLD backward as 'DLROW' with a prolonged pause. Speech is fluent without paraphasia but with occasional word-finding hesitation. Follows simple one-step commands but has difficulty with complex two-step commands.
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: Pupils 3 mm bilaterally, reactive but sluggish. Visual fields full to confrontation. No papilledema on fundoscopic exam. Extraocular movements intact without nystagmus. No facial asymmetry. Hearing grossly intact bilaterally. Palate elevates symmetrically. Tongue midline. No focal cranial nerve deficits identified.
neurological_exam.motor: Strength 5/5 in all four extremities. No pronator drift. No fasciculations or abnormal movements. Tone normal throughout.
neurological_exam.sensory: Light touch and pinprick intact in all dermatomes. Proprioception intact at great toes bilaterally. No sensory level identified.
neurological_exam.reflexes: Biceps 2+ bilaterally, triceps 2+ bilaterally, brachioradialis 2+ bilaterally, patellar 2+ bilaterally, Achilles 1+ bilaterally. Plantar responses flexor bilaterally.
neurological_exam.coordination: Finger-to-nose intact bilaterally. Heel-to-shin intact bilaterally. No dysmetria or dysdiadochokinesia.
neurological_exam.gait: Gait not formally assessed due to patient's reluctance to ambulate, citing severe headache and photophobia. When standing briefly at bedside, appeared steady without lateral drift.
neurological_exam.additional: Marked nuchal rigidity present — passive neck flexion elicits resistance and the patient grimaces. Kernig sign positive bilaterally (hamstring spasm with knee extension when hip is flexed to 90 degrees). Brudzinski sign equivocal — slight hip flexion noted with forced neck flexion. Marked photophobia — requests lights be dimmed. Petechial rash noted on bilateral lower extremities and trunk, approximately 15-20 scattered non-blanching petechiae 1-3 mm in size.

patient.chief_complaint: Worsening headache, confusion, and fever despite 3 days of antibiotics
patient.history_present_illness: Ms. Shanice Washington is a 42-year-old right-handed man with a history of splenectomy following a motor vehicle accident at age 32, hypertension, type 2 diabetes, and recurrent sinusitis who presents to the emergency department with worsening headache, confusion, and fever. Her wife provides the majority of the history as the patient is intermittently confused. She reports that approximately four days ago, she developed nasal congestion, mild headache, and a temperature of 100.2°F. She was seen by her primary care physician the following day, who diagnosed sinusitis and started amoxicillin 500 mg three times daily. For the first two days on antibiotics, her symptoms seemed to plateau — the congestion improved slightly but the headache persisted and she developed intermittent chills. Yesterday evening, her wife noticed she seemed 'not himself,' describing him as slow to answer questions and somewhat disoriented when she got up to use the bathroom, bumping into the doorframe. She attributed this to fatigue and a possible reaction to the antibiotics. This morning, she found him in bed complaining of a severe headache that she rated as 9 out of 10, far worse than anything she had experienced before. She was photophobic, wincing when she opened the blinds, and when she asked him what day it was, she gave the wrong answer. She vomited once while getting dressed. Her temperature at home was 100.8°F. She immediately drove him to the emergency department, arriving at 08:45. In the ED, she reports the headache as predominantly frontal and occipital, constant, and throbbing. She denies any visual changes, weakness, or numbness in her extremities. She has no history of seizures and no witnessed seizure activity. Her wife reports she has not traveled recently, has no known sick contacts other than a student with a cold two weeks ago, and has not had any recent dental procedures. She also notes that she missed her follow-up appointment for pneumococcal vaccine booster that was recommended by her hematologist two years ago.

findings.location: Subtle enhancement along the cerebral convexities bilaterally, most prominent over the frontoparietal regions

signal_characteristics.T1_post_contrast: Thin, linear enhancement along the leptomeninges bilaterally, more prominent on the left frontoparietal convexity
signal_characteristics.FLAIR: Subtle sulcal FLAIR hyperintensity in the bilateral frontoparietal regions, more conspicuous on the left
signal_characteristics.DWI: No frank restricted diffusion in the brain parenchyma; no evidence of cerebritis or abscess formation

findings.mass_effect: No significant mass effect. No midline shift. No hydrocephalus.
findings.borders: Enhancement follows the sulcal pattern, conforming to leptomeningeal surfaces

mri.impression: Subtle leptomeningeal enhancement along the bilateral cerebral convexities, most prominent over the left frontoparietal region, findings suspicious for meningitis in the appropriate clinical context. No evidence of complications such as subdural empyema, cerebritis, brain abscess, or hydrocephalus. Clinical correlation recommended.

labs.interpretation: Abnormal values: WBC 18.7 x10^9/L (H), Neutrophils 82 % (H), Bands 8 % (H), Lymphocytes 4 % (L), Hemoglobin 12.8 g/dL (L), Hematocrit 37.7 % (L), Platelets 139 x10^9/L (L), Sodium 126 mEq/L (L), Bicarbonate 21 mEq/L (L), Glucose 194 mg/dL (H), Calcium 7.9 mg/dL (L), CRP 174 mg/L (H), Procalcitonin 4.8 ng/mL (H), ESR 64 mm/hr (H), Lactate 3.2 mmol/L (H), INR 1.3  (H), Fibrinogen 470 mg/dL (H), D-dimer 2.3 mcg/mL FEU (H), HbA1c 7.4 % (H).

special_tests.Gram_stain: No organisms seen (negative, likely due to prior oral antibiotic therapy)
special_tests.CSF_lactate: 6.8 mmol/L (elevated, reference <3.5 mmol/L; consistent with bacterial meningitis)

csf.interpretation: Opening pressure: 29 cmH2O. WBC: 388 cells/uL (elevated) (72% PMN/24% lymph). Protein: 177.1 mg/dL (elevated, reference 15-45 mg/dL). Glucose: 36.4 mg/dL (low, reference 40-70 mg/dL) (ratio 0.18 (low, serum glucose 188 mg/dL; normal ratio >0.6)). Gram_stain: No organisms seen (negative, likely due to prior oral antibiotic therapy). CSF_latex_agglutination: Positive for Streptococcus pneumoniae antigen. CSF_lactate: 6.8 mmol/L (elevated, reference <3.5 mmol/L; consistent with bacterial meningitis).

special_tests.CSF_culture: No growth at 48 hours (may reflect effective antibiotic therapy)

output.interpretation: Opening pressure: 17 cmH2O. WBC: 80 cells/uL (decreased from 380) (35% PMN/58% lymph). Protein: 93.2 mg/dL (decreased from 168 mg/dL, still elevated). Glucose: 50.1 mg/dL (improved from 34 mg/dL, approaching normal) (ratio 0.38 (improved from 0.18; serum glucose 126 mg/dL)). Gram_stain: No organisms seen. CSF_culture: No growth at 48 hours (may reflect effective antibiotic therapy). CSF_lactate: 3.8 mmol/L (decreased from 6.8 mmol/L, approaching normal).

signal_characteristics.CT: No intracranial hemorrhage, no mass lesion, no midline shift. No hydrocephalus. Gray-white matter differentiation preserved.

additional_observations: No calvarial fracture or bony destruction
output.impression: No acute intracranial abnormality. No hemorrhage, mass, or hydrocephalus to contraindicate lumbar puncture. Clinical correlation recommended.

output.interpretation: Abnormal values: Aerobic Bottle (Set 1) Streptococcus pneumoniae  (H), Aerobic Bottle (Set 2) Streptococcus pneumoniae  (H), Susceptibility - Penicillin MIC 0.12 mcg/mL  (H).

Audiometry.test: Right ear pure tone average (500, 1000, 2000, 4000 Hz)
Left ear pure tone average (500, 1000, 2000, 4000 Hz)

output.interpretation: Abnormal values: Right ear pure tone average (500, 1000, 2000, 4000 Hz) 32 dB HL dB HL (H), Speech discrimination - Right 84%  (H).
abnormal_values_summary: Right ear pure tone average (500, 1000, 2000, 4000 Hz): 32 dB HL dB HL (H)

output.proposed: Ceftriaxone 2g IV every 12 hours plus Vancomycin 1g IV every 12 hours plus Dexamethasone 0.15 mg/kg IV every 6 hours
interactions: Metformin: Should be held during acute illness with IV contrast potential and hemodynamic instability; risk of lactic acidosis in setting of sepsis
Vancomycin and Ceftriaxone: No significant direct interaction; both can be administered concurrently. Monitor renal function with vancomycin.
contraindications: Documented penicillin allergy (childhood rash): Cross-reactivity between penicillin and third-generation cephalosporins (ceftriaxone) is approximately 1-2%. Given that the patient tolerates amoxicillin (a penicillin) without issue, true cephalosporin cross-reactivity is extremely unlikely. Ceftriaxone can be administered with monitoring.
warnings: Dexamethasone should be given before or with the first dose of antibiotics to reduce mortality and hearing loss in pneumococcal meningitis — benefit is diminished if given after antibiotics
Monitor vancomycin trough levels (target 15-20 mcg/mL for meningitis)
Monitor for Clostridioides difficile infection with prolonged antibiotic course
Assess insulin needs given stress hyperglycemia compounded by dexamethasone-induced hyperglycemia
output.formulary_status: Standard empiric regimen for community-acquired bacterial meningitis in adults
alternatives: Meropenem 2g IV every 8 hours (if true cephalosporin allergy)
Chloramphenicol 1g IV every 6 hours (historical alternative, rarely used)
Linezolid (for vancomycin-intolerant patients with resistant organisms)

output.query: partially treated bacterial meningitis CSF findings diagnosis management

results.title: Effect of Prior Antibiotic Treatment on Cerebrospinal Fluid Profiles in Bacterial Meningitis
results.authors: Nigrovic LE, Malley R, Macias CG, et al.
results.key_finding: Prior oral antibiotic treatment can sterilize CSF cultures in up to 44% of cases while CSF cell count, protein, and glucose remain abnormal. Gram stain sensitivity drops from 60-90% to 40-60%.

results.title: Practice Guidelines for the Management of Bacterial Meningitis
results.authors: Tunkel AR, Hartman BJ, Kaplan SL, et al.
results.key_finding: Empiric therapy for community-acquired meningitis in adults 18-50 should include vancomycin plus a third-generation cephalosporin. Adjunctive dexamethasone should be administered before or with the first dose of antibiotics.

results.title: Dexamethasone in Adults with Bacterial Meningitis
results.authors: de Gans J, van de Beek D
results.key_finding: Adjunctive dexamethasone (0.15 mg/kg every 6 hours for 4 days) reduced mortality from 15% to 7% and unfavorable outcomes in pneumococcal meningitis when administered before or with first antibiotic dose.

output.summary: Partially treated bacterial meningitis presents a diagnostic challenge: prior oral antibiotics can sterilize CSF cultures and reduce the sensitivity of Gram stain while CSF chemistries (protein, glucose) and cell counts remain abnormal but may be modified. In this clinical scenario, adjunctive diagnostic tests such as CSF bacterial antigen testing (latex agglutination) and meningitis/encephalitis PCR panels are particularly valuable, as they remain positive despite antibiotic pretreatment. Serum procalcitonin >0.5 ng/mL and CSF lactate >3.5 mmol/L also help distinguish bacterial from viral meningitis in equivocal cases. Empiric treatment should not be delayed and should follow standard guidelines (vancomycin plus ceftriaxone with adjunctive dexamethasone for suspected pneumococcal meningitis in adults).

ground_truth.primary_diagnosis: Acute bacterial meningitis due to Streptococcus pneumoniae (pneumococcal meningitis), partially treated, in an asplenic patient

differential.likelihood: Moderate initially, low after CSF results
differential.key_distinguishing: Viral meningitis typically shows lymphocytic pleocytosis, normal glucose, lower protein, and normal procalcitonin. The mixed pleocytosis here could initially suggest viral etiology but low glucose ratio (0.18), elevated CSF lactate (6.8), and markedly elevated serum procalcitonin (4.8) strongly favor bacterial infection.

HSV encephalitis presents with personality changes, seizures, and temporal lobe involvement on MRI. No temporal lobe abnormality seen on MRI, no seizures, and clinical presentation is more consistent with meningitis than encephalitis.

TB meningitis has a more subacute course (weeks), basilar meningeal enhancement, very low glucose, and lymphocytic predominance. This patient has a more acute course (days), and the CSF pneumococcal antigen is positive.

Cryptococcal meningitis typically occurs in severely immunocompromised patients (HIV/AIDS, transplant). While this patient is asplenic, he is not at high risk for cryptococcal disease. CSF India ink and CrAg would be negative, and PCR panel was negative for Cryptococcus.
Imaging confirms sinusitis, which is likely the source of contiguous spread to the meninges, but the CSF findings, meningeal signs, and systemic toxicity are not explained by sinusitis alone.

optimal_actions.action: Recognize clinical triad of headache, fever, and altered mental status with meningeal signs as a neurological emergency requiring immediate evaluation for meningitis
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Nuchal rigidity, Kernig sign positive, photophobia, confusion, petechial rash

optimal_actions.action: Obtain stat blood cultures (two sets) before initiating antibiotics
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Cultures subsequently positive for Streptococcus pneumoniae in 2/2 sets

optimal_actions.action: Obtain CT head prior to lumbar puncture given altered mental status to exclude mass lesion or elevated ICP
optimal_actions.expected_finding: No acute intracranial pathology, no contraindication to LP, sinus disease identified

optimal_actions.action: Initiate empiric IV antibiotics immediately (ceftriaxone 2g IV plus vancomycin 15-20 mg/kg IV) and adjunctive dexamethasone (0.15 mg/kg IV) — do not delay for LP
Administer dexamethasone before or concurrently with the first dose of antibiotics to reduce mortality and hearing loss in pneumococcal meningitis
Perform lumbar puncture with opening pressure measurement and send CSF for cell count, protein, glucose, Gram stain, culture, and meningitis/encephalitis PCR panel
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Elevated opening pressure, mixed pleocytosis with neutrophil predominance, elevated protein, low glucose, negative Gram stain (partially treated), positive pneumococcal antigen

optimal_actions.action: Obtain stat labs including CBC, BMP, CRP, procalcitonin, lactate, and coagulation studies
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Leukocytosis with left shift, hyponatremia (SIADH), elevated inflammatory markers, elevated lactate

optimal_actions.action: Obtain brain MRI with contrast to assess for meningeal enhancement and complications (empyema, cerebritis, hydrocephalus)
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Subtle leptomeningeal enhancement, no complications, sinus disease as potential source

optimal_actions.action: Recognize the significance of asplenia as a major risk factor for encapsulated organism infection (S. pneumoniae, N. meningitidis, H. influenzae) and lapsed pneumococcal vaccination
De-escalate vancomycin once ceftriaxone susceptibility is confirmed from blood cultures; continue ceftriaxone monotherapy for 10-14 days
Monitor for complications: assess hearing (audiometry), monitor sodium for SIADH, watch for seizures, and repeat LP at 48 hours if clinical improvement is inadequate
Plan vaccination update upon discharge: administer PCV20 followed by PPSV23 at least 8 weeks later, plus MenACWY and MenB if not previously given, given asplenic status

critical_actions: Administer empiric IV antibiotics (ceftriaxone plus vancomycin) immediately — do not delay for imaging or LP results
Administer adjunctive dexamethasone before or with the first dose of antibiotics for suspected pneumococcal meningitis
Obtain blood cultures before initiating IV antibiotics
Perform lumbar puncture with CSF analysis including bacterial antigen testing and PCR panel, especially given negative Gram stain in a partially treated patient
Recognize asplenia as a critical risk factor for overwhelming infection with encapsulated organisms and the importance of inadequate vaccination
Monitor for and manage SIADH (fluid restriction, serial sodium monitoring)
Assess for sensorineural hearing loss as a complication of pneumococcal meningitis
contraindicated_actions: Delaying empiric antibiotics to wait for CT, LP, or culture results — meningitis is a time-critical emergency
Performing LP before CT in a patient with altered mental status without first excluding mass lesion or elevated ICP
Withholding dexamethasone or administering it after antibiotics have already been started (benefit is diminished)
Relying solely on negative Gram stain to exclude bacterial meningitis in a partially treated patient
Continuing oral amoxicillin alone as adequate therapy for suspected bacterial meningitis
Dismissing the presentation as simple sinusitis or viral illness given prior oral antibiotics and worsening course
key_reasoning_points: The triad of headache, fever, and altered mental status in a patient with meningeal signs should prompt immediate evaluation for bacterial meningitis regardless of partial antibiotic treatment
Asplenia is a critical predisposing factor for invasive pneumococcal disease; post-splenectomy patients with fever require urgent evaluation and empiric treatment for encapsulated organisms

Prior oral antibiotic therapy can significantly modify CSF findings: Gram stain may be negative, cultures may be sterilized, and the pleocytosis may be more mixed than the typical neutrophilic predominance seen in untreated bacterial meningitis
CSF bacterial antigen testing (latex agglutination) and PCR panels are essential adjunctive diagnostics in partially treated meningitis, as they remain positive despite prior antibiotic exposure
Elevated serum procalcitonin (>2 ng/mL) and CSF lactate (>3.5 mmol/L) are helpful biomarkers for distinguishing bacterial from viral meningitis, particularly in equivocal cases
The very low CSF-to-serum glucose ratio (0.18) is highly specific for bacterial meningitis and should not be ignored even when other findings are modified by treatment
Adjunctive dexamethasone reduces mortality and hearing loss in pneumococcal meningitis but must be given before or with the first dose of antibiotics to be effective
Petechial rash, while classically associated with meningococcal disease, can also occur in pneumococcal meningitis with bacteremia, especially in asplenic patients

metadata.difficulty_description: Moderate - 55-year-old post-splenectomy patient with headache, low-grade fever, and confusion over 24 hours. Partially treated with oral amoxicillin by PCP before presentation. CSF findings modified by prior antibiotics with negative Gram stain but positive pneumococcal antigen. Mixed pleocytosis could initially suggest viral etiology.
clinical_pearls: Post-splenectomy patients are at lifelong risk for overwhelming post-splenectomy infection (OPSI) with encapsulated organisms, and should maintain up-to-date pneumococcal, meningococcal, and Haemophilus influenzae type b vaccinations
Prior oral antibiotic therapy sterilizes CSF cultures in up to 44% of bacterial meningitis cases; CSF antigen testing and PCR panels are critical in these scenarios
The CSF-to-serum glucose ratio is more useful than absolute CSF glucose — a ratio below 0.4 strongly suggests bacterial meningitis
Adjunctive dexamethasone reduces mortality in pneumococcal meningitis from 15% to 7% when given before or with the first antibiotic dose (de Gans & van de Beek, NEJM 2002)
Sinusitis can serve as a source of contiguous spread to the meninges, and paranasal sinus disease should be evaluated as a potential source in meningitis cases
